body:
As part of this work, it will be necessary to more precisely model the differences between types and type constructors (and, perhaps, in the future, type aliases and structural types).

One way to do this is to rename `TypeName[A]` into `TypeId[A]`, and to equip it with several constructors:

```scala
sealed trait TypeId
object TypeId {
  final case class Name[A](namespace: Namespace, name: String, params: List[TypeName[?]]) extends TypeId // simple types
  final case class Constructor1[F[_]](namespace: Namespace, name: String) extends TypeId // collections
  final case class Constructor2[F[_, _]](namespace: Namespace, name: String) extends TypeId // maps
  // In the future:Alias, Structural, etc.
}
```

Then the `TypeRegistry` can allow you to bind a `TypeId.Constructor1[List]("scala", "List", 1)` to a seq binding.

To make this more type safe we could use polykinds, or perhaps just overload `rebind` to take `TypeId.Name`, `TypeId.Constructor`, etc.

cc @plokhotnyuk

author: jdegoes
body:
Hi Pierre! That's great news!

1. `modifier` is the direct successor to annotations (but being pure data and therefore serializable). However, we are trying to minimize the need for them. So seeing your use cases would help us decide if (a) we need to add more modifiers, or (b) we need to add more operators to Schema so you can achieve what you want without modifiers.
2. There exists `Constructor` which is inside `Binding` for records, which allows you to construct the record directly from its primitive pieces. Note that you have to use `Registers`, and the `Constructor` will require that all values have been placed in the appropriate registers. We can add adapters that box primitives (etc.) in order to more emulate the older API -- I think that would be useful for some applications that don't care about the boxing overhead (the goal is to ensure ZIO Schema 2 codecs directly use registers).

author: ghostdogpr
body:
> 1. `modifier` is the direct successor to annotations (but being pure data and therefore serializable). However, we are trying to minimize the need for them. So seeing your use cases would help us decide if (a) we need to add more modifiers, or (b) we need to add more operators to Schema so you can achieve what you want without modifiers.

I am working on conversion from zio-schema to protobuf IDL and from/to protobuf binary. One constraint I have is I need to be backward compatible with my existing .proto files and binaries. To do that, I had to use annotations to avoid changing the scala types, here's an overview:
```scala
// indicates which field numbers should be skipped because they are reserved
final case class reserved(numbers: Int*) extends StaticAnnotation
// these 2 are used to rename types
final case class prefix(value: String)   extends StaticAnnotation
final case class rename(name: String)    extends StaticAnnotation
// when present, declare the type as a nested type inside the parent message
final case class nested()                extends StaticAnnotation
// force an enum to be a oneof type instead of an enum
final case class oneof()                 extends StaticAnnotation
// used for records with a single field only, use the inner type instead
final case class unwrap()                extends StaticAnnotation
```

> 2. There exists `Constructor` which is inside `Binding` for records, which allows you to construct the record directly from its primitive pieces. Note that you have to use `Registers`, and the `Constructor` will require that all values have been placed in the appropriate registers. We can add adapters that box primitives (etc.) in order to more emulate the older API -- I think that would be useful for some applications that don't care about the boxing overhead (the goal is to ensure ZIO Schema 2 codecs directly use registers).

Some example usage with registers would be nice, maybe with https://github.com/zio/zio-blocks/issues/178 it'll be clearer.

```scala
  implicit val intOptionSchema: Schema[Option[Int]]         = Schema.derived
  implicit val floatSomeSchema: Schema[Some[Float]]         = Schema.derived
  implicit val floatOptionSchema: Schema[Option[Float]]     = Schema.derived
  implicit val longSomeSchema: Schema[Some[Long]]           = Schema.derived
  implicit val longOptionSchema: Schema[Option[Long]]       = Schema.derived
  implicit val doubleSomeSchema: Schema[Some[Double]]       = Schema.derived
  implicit val doubleOptionSchema: Schema[Option[Double]]   = Schema.derived

  implicit def anyRefSomeSchema[A <: AnyRef: Schema]: Schema[Some[A]] = Schema.derived

  implicit def anyRefOptionSchema[A <: AnyRef: Schema]: Schema[Option[A]] = Schema.derived
}
```

Currently, I cannot add it to the `Schema` module because I cannot use macro implementations in the same compilation run that defines them.

To solve the issue I'm going to instantiate them manually in the `Schema` module.

body:
I know that @plokhotnyuk is working on `optic` macro which will derive accessors for user-defined data type.

For now there is `field` macro:

```scala
val age = field[Person](_.age)
```

ZIO DynamoDB would directly use `Optic`, `Lens`, `Prism`, `Traversal`, `Optional`, depending on what type of accessor it wants.

The user can use these same optics for manipulating their data structures -- not just inside DSLs like DynamoDB. 

Also different DSLs can use the same optics (e.g. SQL, DynamoDB, Elastic, etc.) so the user only needs one set of optics.

author: jdegoes
body:
The idea is that multiple mime-types can be supported by multiple formats.

Indeed, we could have `Formats` that is just a Vector on `Format`. 

Why?

Because it seems to me that each mime-type needs its own deriver and type class--that for different mime-types, you would want different type classes and / or derivers.

It would be very clumsy to support, e.g. `application/json` and `text/xml` in the same type class and deriver.

What do you think?

author: 987Nabil
body:
Yes, that is true. But as I said, some mime types have the same encoding.
So lets say I have `application/json` and the old `text/json` that I need to support for some external API.
Should I have two type classes? Or should I have by default `application/json` and be able to add a `text/json` to the instance and just use the same en-/decoding?

author: jdegoes
body: Makes sense. 👍
